startScreenOptions.title: AN24.1-AN24.6 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN24.1-AN24.6.

interactions.label: In a patient with left-sided tension pneumothorax, what mechanism causes the tra

params.question: In a patient with left-sided tension pneumothorax, what mechanism causes the trachea to deviate to the right?

answers.text: Reflex contraction of the right sternocleidomastoid muscle displaces the trachea

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tracheal deviation in tension pneumothorax results from intrathoracic pressure changes, not from neck muscle contraction. The sternocleidomastoid acts on the head and neck, not the intrathoracic trachea.

answers.text: The collapsed left lung retracts, pulling the mediastinum towards the affected side

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While the lung does collapse, the mediastinum is pushed AWAY from the affected side by the trapped air under pressure — not pulled towards it.

answers.text: Compensatory hyperinflation of the right lung pulls the trachea across the midline

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The displacement is driven by positive pressure on the affected side, not by negative pressure or active pulling from the opposite lung.

answers.text: Trapped air under rising pressure in the left pleural cavity pushes the mediastinum to the opposite side

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Rising intrapleural pressure on the affected side displaces the entire mediastinum — including the trachea — contralaterally. This is a mechanical displacement driven by positive pressure that cannot escape.

overallFeedback.feedback: In tension pneumothorax, trapped air builds up under pressure in the affected pleural cavity. This rising pressure pushes the entire mediastinum (trachea, heart, great vessels) away from the affected side — a mechanical displacement, not a reflex or compensatory phenomenon.
Correct! In tension pneumothorax, air enters the pleural cavity but cannot escape, building up pressure that pushes the mediastinum — including the trachea — away from the affected side.

metadata.title: In a patient with left-sided tension pne

interactions.label: What anatomical feature makes segmentectomy — removal of a single bronchopulmona

params.question: What anatomical feature makes segmentectomy — removal of a single bronchopulmonary segment — surgically feasible?

answers.text: Pulmonary veins run between segments rather than within them, allowing clean surgical planes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Each segment has its own dedicated bronchus and artery, while pulmonary veins travel in the intersegmental planes. This lets the surgeon identify the boundaries and remove one segment without disrupting neighbouring segments' venous drainage.

answers.text: Each segment is separated from adjacent segments by a complete pleural septum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: There are no complete pleural septa between adjacent segments within a lobe. Visceral pleura covers the external lung surface, not the intersegmental boundaries.

answers.text: Segments share their arterial supply but have independent venous drainage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: It is the opposite: each segment has its own arterial branch, and the veins are intersegmental — shared at the boundaries rather than contained within individual segments.

answers.text: Each segment is connected to the hilum by a separate lobar bronchus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Each segment receives a segmental bronchus, not a lobar bronchus. Lobar (secondary) bronchi serve entire lobes, each containing multiple segments.

overallFeedback.feedback: Each segment has its own bronchus and artery, but pulmonary veins travel between segments (intersegmental planes). This arrangement creates natural surgical boundaries, making it possible to resect a single segment without damaging adjacent segments or their venous drainage.
Correct! Each bronchopulmonary segment is self-contained with its own bronchus and artery, while pulmonary veins run between segments — creating natural surgical planes for segmentectomy.

interactions.label: What is the anatomical basis for the normal 2–3% physiological right-to-left shu

params.question: What is the anatomical basis for the normal 2–3% physiological right-to-left shunt in healthy lungs?

answers.text: Patent bronchopulmonary anastomoses between bronchial arteries and pulmonary arteries

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While bronchopulmonary anastomoses exist, they are not the primary basis for the normal physiological shunt in healthy lungs.

answers.text: Incomplete septa between pulmonary arterial and venous capillary networks

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The pulmonary capillary bed is a continuous gas-exchange network. There are no incomplete septa causing arteriovenous shunting.

answers.text: Venous blood from smaller bronchi drains into pulmonary veins rather than bronchial veins

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Larger bronchi drain through bronchial veins into the azygos system, but smaller bronchi drain into nearby pulmonary veins. This adds a small volume of deoxygenated blood to the oxygenated return, creating the normal 2–3% shunt.

answers.text: Direct passage of blood through non-ventilated alveoli at the lung bases

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes ventilation-perfusion mismatch, a different mechanism from the anatomical shunt arising from bronchial venous drainage into pulmonary veins.

overallFeedback.feedback: The bronchial circulation supplies the bronchial walls. While larger bronchi drain via bronchial veins into the azygos system, smaller bronchi drain into pulmonary veins instead — adding deoxygenated blood to the oxygenated return and creating the normal 2–3% right-to-left shunt.
Correct! Deoxygenated bronchial venous blood draining into pulmonary veins accounts for the small physiological shunt present in all healthy lungs.
